---
title: "C4 - Proteomics Data Analysis"
output: html_document
---

# C4 - Proteomics Data Analysis

## Useful References
- [limma User Guide](https://www.bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/vignettes/limma/inst/doc/usersguide.pdf)
- [MSstats for mass spectrometry](https://msstats.org/)

## Learning Objectives
In this session we will cover:
- Quantitative mass spectrometry (MS) expression matrices.
- Understanding protein dropouts (missing values).
- Using linear models (`limma`) to calculate differential protein abundance.

## Handling Missing Values
In proteomics, $NAs$ (missing values) are incredibly common because if a protein doesn't hit the detector perfectly due to low abundance, the machinery fails to quantify it. 

You cannot simply turn NAs to 0, because it destroys the statistical variance correctly expected of the sample. Imputation (like K-nearest neighbors or random draws from a normal distribution representing the limit of detection) is often required.

## Setup & Differential Abundance (limma)
While `DESeq2` is for count data (integers), mass spec outputs continuous log-intensities. We thus turn back to linear modeling using `limma`.

```{r setup, eval=FALSE}
library(limma)
library(tidyverse)

# Imagine we have an imputed continuous log-intensity matrix
protein_matrix <- read.csv("imputed_protein_intensities.csv", row.names = 1)

# Build a design matrix representing Treated (1) vs Control (0) samples
design <- model.matrix(~ factor(c(0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1)))

# Fit the linear model to each protein row simultaneously
fit <- lmFit(protein_matrix, design)

# Apply Empirical Bayes to squeeze variances (very helpful for small sample sizes)
fit <- eBayes(fit)

# Output results table!
results <- topTable(fit, coef = 2, number = Inf)
head(results)
```

## Visualizing
Because `limma` outputs standard dataframes with `logFC` and `adj.P.Val` columns, you can pipe these directly into `ggplot2` to make identical Volcano plots just like we did with our RNA-Seq data!
